final_answer: 1. Collect baseline metrics (cache hit/miss, DB reads, CPU, latency) to quantify the regression. 2. Examine cache layer metrics and key generation to see if the new query parameters introduced key fragmentation; ensure keys include all affecting parameters. 3. Profile CPU hot paths to identify extra processing from parameter parsing or serialization. 4. Analyze the new queries with EXPLAIN (ANALYZE) and compare to original statements to detect plan changes or loss of index usage. 5. Add or adjust indexes to cover the new predicates and verify with EXPLAIN. 6. Refactor queries: limit selected columns, add appropriate WHERE clauses, batch reads, and use covering indexes to reduce I/O. 7. After each change, re‑measure cache hit rate, DB read volume, and CPU, iterating until metrics improve. 8. If key granularity remains an issue, adjust cache TTLs or increase cache size to keep the cache warm. 9. Set up monitoring alerts for cache‑miss spikes and DB‑read thresholds to catch regressions early.
